messages.content: "According to Aristotle literature indicates the universal element, I.e., what is true for all times and ages and the particular, I.e., what is true of the men, events, customs, and manners of an age" Why is our town considered a universal work of literature A. both a and b B. the theme of "our town" transcends time and location C. Our Town earned a pulitzer prize D. our town is the most commonly produced play in North America
B. the theme of "our town" transcends time and location

"Our claim, our hope, our despair are in the mind- not in things, not in 'scenery' -thornton Wilder" in only 100 words, explain how this quote relates to how Wilder staged Our Town and why it was unusual for the time period.

Thornton Wilder's quote encapsulates his approach to staging the play "Our Town" and why it was considered unusual for its time period. In the quote, Wilder emphasizes that the essence of the play lies in the minds of the characters, not in the physical elements like scenery. This belief is reflected in the minimalist set design he used for the play, where the stage was often empty or sparsely decorated.

Wilder's decision to focus on the minds of the characters rather than elaborate sets was groundbreaking during the time when realistic and detailed sets were the norm. By stripping away the distractions of elaborate scenery, Wilder placed a greater emphasis on the inner thoughts, emotions, and experiences of the characters.

The minimalist staging allowed the universal themes of life, love, and mortality to take center stage, emphasizing the transcendent and timeless aspects of human existence. Wilder believed that by stripping away external distractions, the audience could better connect with the universal truths and emotions that the play aimed to convey.

In summary, Wilder's choice to stage "Our Town" with minimalist sets aligns with his belief that the essence of the play resides in the minds of the characters. This departure from traditional staging techniques of the time underscores the universal themes and emotions of the play, making it a unique and impactful work.
Thornton Wilder was one of America's first playwrights to successfully fuse _ with emotion A. melodramatic B. modernism C. realism D. expressionism
D. expressionism
which author below is not a contemporary of Thornton Wilder A. richard wright B. william faulkner C. carl sandburg D. samuel clemens
D. Samuel Clemens (Samuel Clemens, also known as Mark Twain, was not a contemporary of Thornton Wilder, as he lived in the 19th century).
the motion pictures offered an escape from the drudgery of everyday life in the 1930s and the _suffered at the hand of the new technology A. drama critics B. audiences C. theater D. playwrights

C. theater (Theater suffered at the hand of the new technology of motion pictures in the 1930s. The popularity of movies as a form of entertainment led to a decline in theater attendance and affected the theater industry negatively.)
in only 100 words, describe how Wilder's experiences studying archaeology in Rome affected his writing

Thornton Wilder's experiences studying archaeology in Rome had a profound influence on his writing. Immersed in the ancient ruins and history of Rome, Wilder gained a deep appreciation for the passage of time and the transient nature of human existence. This perspective is evident in his works, notably in "The Bridge of San Luis Rey" and "Our Town." His studies in archaeology instilled in him a fascination with uncovering the buried stories of the past, and this theme of exploring the mysteries of time and humanity permeates his writing. Additionally, his time in Rome exposed him to different cultures and perspectives, broadening his understanding of human nature and enriching the depth of his characters. Overall, his experiences studying archaeology in Rome infused his writing with a sense of awe for history, a recognition of the ephemeral nature of life, and a profound exploration of the human condition.
an inclination toward literal truth or a visually accurate representation is called A. modernism B. expressionism C. style D. realism
D. realism
most plays in early American theaters were _ performances adapted from European plays A. stylistic B. expressionistic C. modernistic D. melodramatic

D. melodramatic (Most plays in early American theaters were melodramatic performances adapted from European plays. Melodrama was a popular theatrical style of the time, characterized by exaggerated emotions, clear-cut moral distinctions, and sensational plotlines.)

the movement in fine arts during the latter part of the nineteenth and early part of the 20th centuries the originated in Europe and emphasized the objective expression of inner experience is called _ A. modernism B. melodrama C. expressionism D. style

C. expressionism (The movement in fine arts during the latter part of the nineteenth and early part of the 20th centuries that originated in Europe and emphasized the objective expression of inner experience is called expressionism.)
the 20th-century thought, character, ideas, or practice, particularly when discussing usage or style is called A. melodrama B. expressionism C. realism D. modernism
D. modernism
in 5-6 sentences explain hypnotic induction, suggestibility, and amnesia

Hypnotic induction is a process used in hypnotherapy to guide individuals into a state of hypnosis. It involves the use of techniques such as relaxation, focused attention, and suggestion to induce a trance-like state in a person's mind. During this process, the hypnotist establishes a deep sense of relaxation and openness in the individual, preparing them for heightened suggestibility.

Suggestibility is a key component of hypnosis, referring to the individual's responsiveness to suggestions given by the hypnotist. In the state of heightened suggestibility, individuals are more open to accepting and acting upon suggestions provided to them. The power of suggestion is used to influence behavior, thoughts, and emotions during hypnotherapy sessions.

Amnesia can sometimes occur as a result of hypnotic induction and suggestibility. In certain cases, the hypnotist may intentionally induce amnesia for specific events or suggestions, causing the individual to forget certain aspects of their experience during hypnosis. This can be useful in addressing traumatic memories or negative thought patterns, allowing the individual to move forward without the emotional burden tied to those memories.

Overall, hypnotic induction, suggestibility, and amnesia are interconnected concepts used in hypnotherapy to create a trance-like state, enhance responsiveness to suggestions, and potentially induce selective forgetfulness for therapeutic purposes.
in 3-4 sentences describe the early uses of hypnosis. how was it used to address psychological symptoms.

In its early uses, hypnosis was primarily seen as a tool for entertainment and mesmerizing audiences. However, in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, hypnosis started being used for therapeutic purposes. It was employed as a way to address psychological symptoms by accessing and influencing the subconscious mind. Hypnosis was used to facilitate psychoanalysis, uncover repressed memories, alleviate pain, manage phobias, and treat conditions like hysteria and anxiety disorders. It was believed that by tapping into the subconscious, hypnosis could help uncover and resolve underlying psychological issues.
in just a few sentences, discuss why some people are easier to hypnotize than others, in 5 sentences.

The ease of hypnotizing individuals can vary due to several factors. Firstly, susceptibility to hypnotic induction is influenced by a person's natural level of suggestibility, which can differ among individuals. Some people have a greater ability to focus their attention, relax, and become absorbed in the hypnotic experience, making them more responsive to hypnotic suggestions. Additionally, personality traits like openness and willingness to engage in imaginative experiences may make a person more susceptible to hypnosis. Factors like trust in the hypnotist, comfort in the hypnotic setting, and belief in the efficacy of hypnosis can also play a role in an individual's responsiveness to hypnosis. Lastly, previous experiences with hypnosis and familiarity with trance-like states can impact an individual's receptiveness to hypnotic induction.
identify how hypnosis is used for pain control and/or psychotherapy. in just a few sentences

Hypnosis is utilized for pain control by inducing a relaxed state and suggesting reduction or elimination of pain sensations. It can be applied in various medical procedures and chronic pain management. In psychotherapy, hypnosis is employed to access the subconscious mind, promote self-awareness, and assist in addressing psychological issues such as anxiety, phobias, and trauma. The hypnotic state allows for exploration of emotions, memories, and underlying beliefs, enabling therapeutic interventions and positive behavioral changes.
